segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The morning after a siege sounds like a town remembering how to breathe. Somewhere a hammer worked at timber -- the gate Thornik had repaired, being reinforced now by the local smith with less skill and more nails.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: A child cried, then stopped -- comforted or exhausted, impossible to tell. The smell of fresh bread moved through smoke-tinged air from a bakehouse that had stayed lit through everything, because bread doesn't care about thralls.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Across the yard, a woman was sweeping the square's south corner with the focused determination of someone who had decided that if she could make one small section of the world clean, the rest would follow. The broom moved in short, fierce strokes.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She didn't look at the scorch marks. She swept around them.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: They were checking tack and repacking what hadn't burned in the stable yard when Caelin said,

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "Nyx was summoned. She left word that she doesn't know when -- or if -- she'll be back."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The words landed in different ways on different people.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Vex set down a saddlebag with more care than usual -- her ribs announcing themselves with every twist.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "Wonderful. Fewer purple fireworks and unsolicited philosophy."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She pulled a strap tight and checked the buckle twice. A beat passed. Softer, grudging:

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "Also fewer brain-scrambles when death squads show up, so... I suppose I'll miss the asset. A little."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She slid a dagger home.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "Don't tell her I said that."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Thornik scratched his beard with a knuckle, eyes on the middle distance.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "Wouldn't dare. She'd charge us for sentiment."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Serana only nodded, eyes already on the road that led east. She'd been looking at it since first light. Not longingly -- assessingly. The way you look at a road you know leads somewhere you need to go and are afraid to arrive.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "We keep moving."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Durgan said nothing. He stood at the yard's edge, blades already cleaned and sheathed, shadow flat and obedient at his feet in the morning sun. If the news moved him, it moved him in a place no one could see.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: But his eyes went once to the lane where Nyxara had walked, and something in his face -- not expression, exactly, but the ghost of an expression -- suggested he understood leaving better than most.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: They worked in quiet for a while -- the productive, necessary quiet of people packing for a journey they're not sure they're coming back from. Rolled blankets, counted coin, checked the edge on steel that had been used the night before and would be used again.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Across the yard, Aldric was inventorying his medical bag with the thorough patience of someone who'd learned the cost of discovering a shortage at the wrong moment. Needle count. Thread count. Three types of bandage, rolled tight as coin stacks.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: A leather pouch of dried herbs he could identify by smell with his eyes closed, and did, because the lighting was poor and he trusted his nose more than his eyes.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He didn't participate in the conversation. He listened the way old soldiers listen -- head down, hands busy, cataloguing everything. The way they moved. The way they talked. What they said and what they didn't.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He was assembling a picture of this group the way he assembled a trauma assessment: systematically, without judgment, noting what worked and what was about to fail.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: What he saw: a party that had been seven and was now six and carried the weight of seven. A hedge-knight with a relic in his arm and a sense of duty that would get him killed if nobody watched.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: A thief who fought like a soldier and cared like a mother and would deny both under oath. A dwarf whose heart was too big for his chest and whose toolkit was too empty for his skills. A paladin whose faith had broken and whose spine had not.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: A shadow-cursed soldier who was losing a fight nobody else could see. And -- watching from the gap Nyxara had left -- an empty space where a warlock with darkening fingers had stood, filled now with morning light and absence.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He'd seen worse groups in better shape. He'd seen better groups in worse shape. He'd work with what was there.
